attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about white patches on her skin that have been gradually appearing over the past eight months. She first noticed them around her knuckles on her hands, but they have since appeared on her axilla, knees and elbows. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. There is no family history of pigmentation changes. She started boxing one year ago and initially thought it was due to trauma from boxing. She denies any pruiritis.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are well-demarcated, depigmented patches with normal skin texture distributed symmetrically around the dorsal aspects of her hands, and over her knees, axilla and elbows. Wood's lamp examination shows sharp demarcation of the patches with chalky white fluorescence.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation is classic for vitiligo, with well-demarcated depigmented patches showing a symmetrical distribution in typical locations. The gradual onset, normal skin texture, and enhancement under Wood's light are characteristic features.

Vitiligo is an acquired autoimmune condition causing destruction of melanocytes, leading to complete loss of pigmentation in affected areas. Vitiligo can occur at trauma areas and sun-exposed areas but also on palms, soles and oral mucosa. Management is often difficult and psychological impacts can be profound.

Tinea versicolor typically presents with hypopigmented or hyperpigmented patches with fine scale, most commonly on the trunk. The patches are usually asymmetrical and do not show the same degree of depigmentation as vitiligo. Under Wood's light, tinea versicolor shows yellow-golden fluorescence due to the presence of Malassezia species.

Pityriasis alba presents with poorly defined hypopigmented patches primarily on the face of children and young adults. The patches are usually scaly and do not show complete depigmentation. They tend to be more prominent in summer months and often resolve spontaneously.

Post-inflammatory hypopigmentation occurs following inflammation or injury to the skin. Whilst boxing could leave to injury over the metacarpophalangeal joints if gloves or taping was not performed, it would not explain the other sites of hypopigmentation. The patches would also have an irregular distribution corresponding to the sites of previous inflammation.

Leprosy can cause hypopigmented patches but these are typically associated with reduced or absent sensation, thickened nerves, and other systemic symptoms. The patches in leprosy are usually asymmetrical and may have altered texture or decreased sweating.
learningPoints: Vitiligo presents as well-demarcated, depigmented patches that are more noticeable in darker skin.
Symmetrical distribution of lesions is characteristic of generalised vitiligo, the most common form.
references: Joge RR, Kathane PU, Joshi SH. Vitiligo: A Narrative Review. Cureus. 2022. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9586189/

Alk J et al. Estimating the burder of vitiligo: a systematic review and modelling study. The Lancet Public Health. 2024: 9 (6). Available at: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanpub/article/PIIS2468-2667(24)00026-4/fulltext
Aslayaydeh B, Amman J, Oakley A. Vitiligo. DermNet NZ. 2022. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/vitiligo
